exercises.title: String operation complexities
exercises.instructions:
State Big-O for each Java String/StringBuilder operation:
A: `s.length()`
B: `s.charAt(5)`
C: `s.substring(2, 7)`
D: `s.equals(t)` (s and t both length n)
E: `s.contains(t)` (s length n, t length m)
F: `sb.append(c)` on StringBuilder
exercises.expected_solution:
A: s.length()          → O(1) — stored as a field
B: s.charAt(5)         → O(1) — direct array index
C: s.substring(2,7)   → O(5) = O(k) where k=r-l — copies k chars
                          In a loop: O(n×k) total → expensive!
D: s.equals(t)         → O(n) — char by char comparison (up to n)
E: s.contains(t)       → O(n×m) — naive substring search
                          s.indexOf(t) is the same O(n×m) naive algorithm
                          KMP would do it in O(n+m) — see T2!
F: sb.append(c)        → O(1) amortized — buffer doubles when full
                          Actual per-op: O(1) usually, O(n) on resize (rare)
                          Amortized across n appends: O(n) total

test_cases.expected:
A: s.length()          → O(1) — stored as a field
B: s.charAt(5)         → O(1) — direct array index
C: s.substring(2,7)   → O(5) = O(k) where k=r-l — copies k chars
                          In a loop: O(n×k) total → expensive!
D: s.equals(t)         → O(n) — char by char comparison (up to n)
E: s.contains(t)       → O(n×m) — naive substring search
                          s.indexOf(t) is the same O(n×m) naive algorithm
                          KMP would do it in O(n+m) — see T2!
F: sb.append(c)        → O(1) amortized — buffer doubles when full
                          Actual per-op: O(1) usually, O(n) on resize (rare)
                          Amortized across n appends: O(n) total

hints: charAt: O(1) — string backed by char[]
substring: copies r-l chars → O(r-l)
StringBuilder.append: amortized O(1)

exercises.title: String == vs equals
exercises.expected_solution:
Output:
true     ← a and b both point to same String pool entry 'hello'
false    ← c = new String(...) creates new heap object, different reference
true     ← equals() compares content ('hello' == 'hello') not references
true     ← equalsIgnoreCase: case-insensitive content comparison

Key rules:
  String literals ("hello") → String pool → same object if same content
  new String("hello")       → always new heap object
  == on Strings: compares references (almost always WRONG for content)
  equals(): ALWAYS use for String content comparison
  Never use == for String comparison in production code!

test_cases.expected:
Output:
true     ← a and b both point to same String pool entry 'hello'
false    ← c = new String(...) creates new heap object, different reference
true     ← equals() compares content ('hello' == 'hello') not references
true     ← equalsIgnoreCase: case-insensitive content comparison

Key rules:
  String literals ("hello") → String pool → same object if same content
  new String("hello")       → always new heap object
  == on Strings: compares references (almost always WRONG for content)
  equals(): ALWAYS use for String content comparison
  Never use == for String comparison in production code!

hints: Literals share pool; new String() is always distinct object
== checks identity (same object), equals() checks value
Rule: always use .equals() for Strings

exercises.title: Benchmark String concat vs StringBuilder
exercises.instructions:
Demonstrate the performance difference:
1. Build string by concatenating n random chars using += (String)
2. Build same string using StringBuilder
3. Benchmark both at n=50,000
4. Print ratio of times
hints: String += at n=50K: ~1250M char copies (n²/2)
StringBuilder: 50K appends, O(1) each
Both produce identical output strings

exercises.title: Reverse a string — 3 ways
exercises.instructions:
Implement string reversal 3 ways and compare:
1. StringBuilder.reverse(): O(n) built-in
2. char[] two-pointer: O(n) manual
3. Recursive: O(n) time, O(n) space (bad — avoid)

All should produce same output for 'Zerodha'.
hints: String immutable: reversal requires char[] or StringBuilder
StringBuilder.reverse(): one line, O(n)
Recursive + substring: looks elegant but actually O(n²)

exercises.title: Valid Anagram using frequency array (LeetCode #242)
exercises.instructions:
Check if two strings are anagrams. O(n) time, O(1) space using int[26].

is_anagram('anagram', 'nagaram') → true
is_anagram('rat', 'car') → false

NO sorting. NO HashMap. Use int[26] frequency array.
hints: freq[c-'a']++/-- : increment for s, decrement for t
If any freq[i]!=0: chars don't balance → not anagram
int[26] size is constant (26 letters) → O(1) space

exercises.title: Roman to Integer (LeetCode #13)
exercises.instructions:
Convert Roman numeral string to integer.
IV=4, IX=9, XL=40, XC=90, CD=400, CM=900
Otherwise add normally.

'III'=3, 'IV'=4, 'MCMXCIV'=1994
hints: curr < next: subtraction (IV=4, IX=9). curr >= next: addition
charAt(i): O(1) access
Map of 7 symbols: O(1) lookup

exercises.title: String to Integer — atoi (LeetCode #8)
exercises.instructions:
Implement atoi. Skip leading spaces, handle sign, read digits, clamp to [INT_MIN, INT_MAX].

'   -42' → -42, '4193 with words' → 4193, 'words 987' → 0, '91283472332' → INT_MAX
hints: Use long to avoid overflow during digit accumulation
Clamp: check against Integer.MAX/MIN_VALUE before returning
Character.isDigit(): returns true for '0'-'9'

exercises.title: Longest Common Prefix (LeetCode #14)
exercises.instructions:
Find longest common prefix string amongst array of strings.

strs=['flower','flow','flight'] → 'fl'
strs=['dog','racecar','car'] → ''

O(n×m) where n=strings, m=avg length.
hints: Start with strs[0] as prefix, shrink it for each subsequent string
startsWith: check if string begins with prefix
Empty prefix means no common prefix

exercises.title: Count and Say (LeetCode #38)
exercises.instructions:
Generate the nth term of Count-and-Say sequence:
1→'1', 2→'11', 3→'21', 4→'1211', 5→'111221'

Each term describes the previous: '21' = 'one 2, one 1'.
Generate term n iteratively using StringBuilder.
hints: StringBuilder for building next term: avoid O(n²) concatenation
Count consecutive same chars using inner while loop
Append count then char: sb.append(count).append(c)

exercises.title: Hidden O(n²) in String operations
exercises.instructions:
For each code block, identify if it's O(n) or O(n²) and explain why:

A:
```java
String s = input; // length n
String result = "";
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) result += s.charAt(i);
```
B:
```java
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(input);
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) sb.append(s.charAt(i));
```
C:
```java
String s = input; // length n
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) System.out.println(s.substring(0, i));
```
D:
```java
String[] words = input.split(" "); // n words, avg length m
String joined = String.join("-", words);
```
exercises.expected_solution:
A: O(n²) — String += in loop
   result starts empty. At step i, result has i chars.
   result += s.charAt(i) copies all i chars + 1 = O(i) per step.
   Total: 1+2+...+n = n(n+1)/2 = O(n²)
   Fix: use StringBuilder

B: O(n) — StringBuilder.append
   sb already has 'input' (n chars) at start.
   Each sb.append(s.charAt(i)) is O(1) amortized.
   Total: O(n) for n appends + O(n) for initial constructor.
   But: we're appending n MORE chars to an already-n string.
   Still O(n) amortized total.

C: O(n²) — substring(0,i) in loop
   s.substring(0, i) copies i characters.
   At step i: O(i) for substring + O(i) for println.
   Total: 2×(1+2+...+n) = O(n²)
   Fix: use indices instead, print s.charAt(i)

D: O(n×m) — split + join both O(total chars)
   split: scans all n×m total chars once = O(n×m)
   String.join: copies all n×m chars once = O(n×m)
   Total: O(n×m) which is O(total input length)
   This is optimal — you must read all characters at least once

test_cases.expected:
A: O(n²) — String += in loop
   result starts empty. At step i, result has i chars.
   result += s.charAt(i) copies all i chars + 1 = O(i) per step.
   Total: 1+2+...+n = n(n+1)/2 = O(n²)
   Fix: use StringBuilder

B: O(n) — StringBuilder.append
   sb already has 'input' (n chars) at start.
   Each sb.append(s.charAt(i)) is O(1) amortized.
   Total: O(n) for n appends + O(n) for initial constructor.
   But: we're appending n MORE chars to an already-n string.
   Still O(n) amortized total.

C: O(n²) — substring(0,i) in loop
   s.substring(0, i) copies i characters.
   At step i: O(i) for substring + O(i) for println.
   Total: 2×(1+2+...+n) = O(n²)
   Fix: use indices instead, print s.charAt(i)

D: O(n×m) — split + join both O(total chars)
   split: scans all n×m total chars once = O(n×m)
   String.join: copies all n×m chars once = O(n×m)
   Total: O(n×m) which is O(total input length)
   This is optimal — you must read all characters at least once

hints: String +=: copies existing string each step → triangular sum = O(n²)
substring(0,i): copies i chars each call
split/join: linear in total character count

exercises.title: Decode String (LeetCode #394)
exercises.instructions:
Decode encoded string: k[encoded_string] means repeat encoded_string k times.

's=3[a]2[bc]' → 'aaabcbc'
's=3[a2[c]]' → 'accaccacc'

Use stack — O(n) time and space.
hints: Two stacks: one for repeat counts, one for string contexts
'[': push current state, reset for nested content
']': pop, repeat current content, restore previous context

exercises.title: Zigzag conversion (LeetCode #6)
exercises.instructions:
Write 'PAYPALISHIRING' in zigzag pattern with numRows=3:
P   A   H   N
A P L S I I G
Y   I   R
Read row by row: 'PAHNAPLSIIGYIR'

O(n) using StringBuilder per row.
hints: dir: starts -1, flips at row 0 and row numRows-1
row += dir: bounces between 0 and numRows-1
Concatenate all rows at end

exercises.title: String compression (LeetCode #443)
exercises.instructions:
Compress char array in-place: 'aabcccccaaa' → 'a2bc5a3' (array becomes ['a','2','b','c','5','a','3'])
Return new length. Must be O(1) extra space.

Run-length encoding in-place using slow/fast two pointers.
hints: write: where to write next compressed char
read: scans and counts consecutive same chars
String.valueOf(count).toCharArray(): handles multi-digit counts

exercises.title: Simplify Unix Path (LeetCode #71)
exercises.instructions:
Simplify a Unix path string:
'/home/' → '/home'
'/a/./b/../../c/' → '/c'
'/../' → '/'

Split by '/', process components using Deque as stack.
hints: split('/'): handles multiple slashes, produces empty strings (skip them)
'..': pop from stack (go up). '.': skip. name: push
Build path by reversing stack

exercises.title: Complete M31 T1 String Fundamentals Synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete string toolkit:
1. reverseWords(String s): reverse word order, trim extra spaces
2. isPalindrome(String s): O(1) space two-pointer, alphanumeric only
3. frequencyMap(String s): int[26] char frequencies
4. compressRunLength(String s): 'aaabbc' → 'a3b2c1'
5. All 4 tests pass
hints: reverseWords: split on \\s+ (handles multiple spaces)
isPalindrome: skip non-alphanumeric with inner while
compressRunLength: StringBuilder, count consecutive same chars
